title: Rep. Alan Seabaugh and the Louisiana House Ensure Murder by Abortion Continues
author: James Silberman
orientation: An abolitionist-perspective news report on how Rep. Alan Seabaugh's gutting amendment, plus pressure from pro-life establishment groups, replaced the immediately-effective Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act (HB813) with redundant trigger language. The article frames the vote as evidence that the pro-life establishment, not just pro-choice opposition, is the obstacle to abolition.
audiences: Pro-life voters reassessing their political alliances
Pastors and church leaders considering equal-protection theology

questions.question: What did the Louisiana House actually do to HB813 on May 12, 2022?
questions.answer: The House adopted an amendment that stripped HB813 of its abolition language and substituted trigger language that merely raised penalties slightly above an existing 2006 trigger law, turning the bill into a duplicate of Senate Bill 342 and leading the author to pull it from consideration.
questions.quote: The Louisiana State House voted on Thursday to strike all language from the Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act (HB813) and replace it with trigger language despite a trigger bill already having been passed in 2006.

questions.question: Who filed the gutting amendment, and how is that politically significant?
questions.answer: The amendment was filed by self-described pro-life Rep. Alan Seabaugh, who had voted enthusiastically for the unamended bill in committee just days before, illustrating the article's claim that the danger to abolition comes from inside the pro-life caucus.
questions.quote: The amendment that gutted the bill was filed by pro-life State Rep. Alan Seabaugh, who enthusiastically supported HB813 as originally written in the committee hearing.

questions.question: How did the final vote break down?
questions.answer: Sixty-five representatives voted to adopt the gutting amendment and twenty-six voted against it, with many of the same Republicans who had advanced the bill out of committee voting to erase it on the floor.
questions.quote: Many of the same Republicans who voted the bill out of committee voted to adopt the amendment that erased the bill they voted for, with the final tally being 65 voting in favor of gutting the bill and 26 against.

questions.question: What constitutional argument did Seabaugh use to justify the amendment?
questions.audience: Pro-life voters reassessing their political alliances
questions.answer: Seabaugh argued that Roe v. Wade still controls and that Louisiana cannot abolish abortion without the Supreme Court's permission, a claim abolitionists reject as judicial supremacy that hands lawmaking power to unelected judges.
questions.quote: He continued by arguing that *Roe v. Wade* is "still the law" and that we cannot pass a law to abolish abortion without the supreme court's permission.

questions.question: What did Seabaugh allegedly misrepresent about the bill's text?
questions.answer: Seabaugh claimed the bill could expose women to retroactive prosecution for past abortions, but Section 5 of the bill explicitly limits its application to crimes committed on or after the effective date.
questions.quote: On the contrary, section 5 of the bill establishes that "This Act applies to crimes committed on or after the effective date of this 21 Act. For purposes of this Act, a crime is committed before the effective date of this Act if 22 any element of the crime occurs before the effective date."

questions.question: How did spectators in the chamber react to Seabaugh's floor speech?
questions.answer: Audience members in the gallery openly accused him of lying, signaling that abolitionists watching the proceedings believed his characterization of the bill was knowingly false.
questions.quote: Shouts of "liar" erupted from the balcony of the chamber as Seabaugh mislead his colleagues and everyone across the nation watching the bill.

questions.question: What position did Louisiana Right to Life take on prosecuting mothers?
questions.audience: Pro-life voters reassessing their political alliances
questions.answer: LARTL issued a press release defending its longstanding policy that women who obtain abortions should not be treated as criminals, a stance the article rejects as inconsistent with treating preborn children as persons under equal protection.
questions.quote: Our longstanding policy is that abortion-vulnerable women should not be treated as criminals.

questions.question: How did Governor John Bel Edwards weigh in on HB813?
questions.answer: Edwards, a self-identified pro-life Democrat, publicly dismissed the idea of jailing women for abortion as absurd, which the article uses as evidence that bipartisan pro-life rhetoric still protects the act of abortion itself.
questions.quote: To suggest that women would be jailed for an abortion is simply absurd.

questions.question: What was the role of Congressman Mike Johnson in the bill's defeat?
questions.audience: Pro-life voters reassessing their political alliances
questions.answer: Johnson personally lobbied state legislators by phone to vote against HB813, demonstrating that prominent national pro-life figures actively intervened to stop abolition at the state level.
questions.quote: Pro-life Congressman Mike Johnson also played a significant role in the effort to prevent abortion from being immediately abolished in Louisiana, making phone calls to key state legislators telling them to oppose the bill.

questions.question: How did the broader pro-life coalition position itself the day of the vote?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders considering equal-protection theology
questions.answer: Seventy national pro-life organizations released a joint letter while the House was deliberating, opposing any criminal penalty for women who procure abortion no matter the number of children killed.
questions.quote: 70 leading pro-life organizations came out with a statement on Thursday — while the Louisiana House was deliberating — opposing any penalties for mothers no matter how many preborn children they intentionally murder.

questions.question: What concrete actions does the article ask readers to take after the bill's defeat?
questions.answer: Readers are urged to join the email list, lobby their state senator and representative, request help from Free the States, and spread abolitionist content to convert pro-lifers within their own circles.
questions.quote: Lobby your State Senator and Representative regularly and feel free to [send us a message](https://freethestates.org/contact/index.html) if you would like help with doing so.

questions.question: How does the author frame the responsibility for abortion continuing in Louisiana?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders considering equal-protection theology
questions.answer: Silberman places blame squarely on pro-life organizations for teaching legislators to oppose total abolition, arguing the body count is a direct consequence of their lobbying.
questions.quote: The result of these pro-life groups teaching legislators that they should oppose abortion's total abolition is babies continuing to be murdered in Louisiana.

related.title: Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act Passes Committee!
related.reason: Direct prequel covering HB813's committee passage just over a week before the floor vote that gutted it.

related.title: "I am a pro-life Oklahoman who voted to table a bill that would abolish all abortion"
related.reason: Parallel case study of a pro-life legislator killing an abolition bill, mirroring Seabaugh's flip on HB813.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Lays out the abolitionist critique of trigger bills and incremental measures like the one substituted for HB813.

related.title: National Right to Life Endorses Greg McCortney, the Senator Who Kept Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Reinforces the article's thesis that national pro-life groups actively reward politicians who block abolition.

related.title: Never Be Impressed By a Politician's Claim to Be "100% Pro-Life"
related.reason: Provides the framework for evaluating self-styled pro-life Republicans like Seabaugh whose votes contradict their labels.

related.title: WATCH: How the Seven-Pronged Pro-Life Political Strategy Has Failed
related.reason: Background on why the trigger-law strategy substituted into HB813 is, by abolitionist analysis, designed to delay rather than end abortion.

related.title: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
related.reason: Explores the institutional hostility from groups like LARTL and NRLC that drove the lobbying campaign against HB813.
